weather.title: Metro Manila Weather
weather.sourceNote: Source: PAGASA pattern — hot dry season; isolated afternoon convection possible

exchangeRate.title: PHP:USD Exchange Rate
exchangeRate.trend: ▼ Peso among Asia’s weakest in March on oil + Fed
exchangeRate.contextHtml: Week in FX: After breaking P60, the peso stayed under pressure — trading near ₱60.03 (Mar 20) and weakening further to about ₱60.34/USD by mid-week as Middle East risk and hawkish Fed expectations fed import costs. BSP intervened to moderate sharp swings rather than defend a floor. Gold volatility added secondary noise for savers.
exchangeRate.sourceNote: Source: BSP daily FX / Trading Economics / scan week 2026-03-22–29

articles.headline: DSWD Rolls Out ₱5,000 Cash Aid to 34,000+ Metro Manila Transport Workers
articles.descriptionHtml: The Department of Social Welfare and Development opened a rolling payout under AICS: TNVS drivers on March 24, jeepney drivers March 25, delivery riders March 26-27, and motorcycle taxi riders March 28. President Marcos highlighted related distributions to Metro Manila drivers as emergency response to the Middle East–driven fuel shock (crude quoted above $100/bbl; peso near ₱60.03).
articles.significance: Formalizes crisis cash relief beyond fare/toll measures — a political and welfare signal that transport livelihoods are under sustained pressure.
articles.diasporaImpact: Families with TNVS, rider, or jeepney income may see short-term relief; one-off ₱5K is thin against volatile pump prices.

articles.headline: Peso Weakness Persists Past ₱60 — Among Asia’s Worst March Performers
articles.descriptionHtml: After breaking ₱60, the peso slid to about ₱60.03/USD (Mar 20 context) and media reported a fresh low near ₱60.34 by March 23. Drivers include expensive crude, Middle East risk, and hawkish Fed expectations. BSP steered toward smoothing sharp moves rather than defending a floor; the currency weakened more than 4% in March, hurting import purchasing power even as remittances convert at weaker rates.
articles.significance: Sustained FX stress keeps macro policy and social assistance in the spotlight as election-cycle politics heat up.
articles.diasporaImpact: More pesos per dollar helps on paper; food, fuel, and logistics inflation eats into real household budgets back home.

articles.headline: Fuel Volatility, Toll Discounts for PUVs, and Growing Calls for Fuel-Tax Relief
articles.descriptionHtml: Throughout the week, reporting framed energy as ongoing volatility rather than a one-day oil print: toll-fee discounts for PUVs, buses, and freight began March 23 on major expressways; lawmakers renewed pushes for fuel-tax suspension and targeted aid (e.g. DZRH, Mar 27). Agencies kept contingency talking points—anti-profiteering, subsidies, possible excise relief—as CAB maintained an elevated fuel surcharge tier for aviation, keeping ticket prices sensitive to energy swings.
articles.significance: The story is governance credibility under cost-of-living stress, not whether any single trading session was “up” or “down.”
articles.diasporaImpact: Balikbayan flights, shipping, and pasalubong logistics stay price-fragile; families feel sticker shock before headline CPI fully reflects it.

articles.headline: Pump-Price Math Remains Brutal for Households
articles.descriptionHtml: Explainers in circulation (e.g. GMA, early March context recycled in late-March scans) underline how DOE-tracked adjustments can move gasoline, diesel, and kerosene by large peso amounts per liter when global benchmarks swing—reinforcing why DSWD transport aid and tax-relief debates stayed central through month-end.
articles.significance: Deregulated pricing means political tolerance for pain at the pump is a real regime risk for any administration.
articles.diasporaImpact: Remitters may need to top up support when provincial neighbors and urban relatives face synchronized fuel-and-food shocks.

articles.headline: VP Sara Duterte Impeachment — March 25 “Mini-Trial,” Answer Filed, Senate Dismissal Bid
articles.descriptionHtml: Ahead of March 25, the House Committee on Justice framed a rules-based “mini-trial”: Chair Gerville Luistro urged the Vice President to appear and answer charges (confidential funds, alleged fraudulent liquidation, DepEd-era conduct, and reported threats against President Marcos and family); Rep. Terry Ridon argued procedure would survive Supreme Court review. March 23 reporting emphasized complaints sufficient in form and substance, with Duterte’s March 16 answer criticized for not engaging the merits—while she accused the House of double standards vs. a prior Marcos impeachment bid. Later in the week, her camp also sought Senate dismissal of the complaint as defective, keeping the Marcos–Duterte rupture inside institutions.
articles.significance: Probable-cause dynamics and attendance choices this week can accelerate transmission to a Senate trial—or trigger sharper legal confrontations.
articles.diasporaImpact: 2028 coalition math and stability of civilian-military-business confidence ride on how this process is perceived abroad.

articles.headline: ICC Duterte Case — Confirmation Hearing Done; Decision Due Within ~60 Days
articles.descriptionHtml: The ICC announced conclusion of the confirmation of charges hearing for former President Rodrigo Duterte after February 27, 2026, starting a clock for a Pre-Trial Chamber decision (typically within about 60 days—watch late April). Separately, on March 6, the Appeals Chamber rejected his detention appeal, leaving him in The Hague custody as Philippine domestic politics and the VP impeachment track run in parallel.
articles.significance: A confirmed case would move Duterte toward a full Trial Chamber—a landmark for Southeast Asia and for accountability politics at home.
articles.diasporaImpact: Human-rights networks overseas have pushed ICC engagement for years; the ruling will echo in coalition politics ahead of 2028.

articles.headline: West Philippine Sea — Fire-Control Radar on BRP Miguel Malvar; PCG Denies Secret China Deal
articles.descriptionHtml: Manila confirmed a PLA Navy unit (bow 622) trained fire-control radar on the BRP Miguel Malvar near Sabina Shoal on March 7, publicized around March 21-22. Officials condemned the lock-on as dangerous escalation. The Philippine Coast Guard separately denied reports of a clandestine maritime cooperation pact with Beijing as lawmakers pressed for scrutiny of Chinese interference, including alleged jamming over WPS patrol lanes.
articles.significance: Radar paints are treated as near-hostile acts; they test alliance signaling with Washington and domestic resolve on fishery and energy security.
articles.diasporaImpact: Coastal provinces and seafaring OFWs face higher strategic risk; diaspora advocacy on maritime rights remains active in US and EU capitals.

articles.headline: Kanlaon Unrest Still on Watchlists
articles.descriptionHtml: After March’s earlier eruptive episodes, monitors flagged Mt. Kanlaon as still meriting vigilance entering the week of March 22—with PHIVOLCS continuing assessments and communities in Negros advised to heed ash and lahar protocols.
articles.significance: Repeated 2026 activity keeps disaster resilience spending and farm-sector stability in focus.
articles.diasporaImpact: Negrense families abroad often bankroll evacuation, medical, and farm recovery costs during volcanic upticks.

articles.headline: President Marcos Narrows Approval Gap with Vice President Duterte
articles.descriptionHtml: Survey reporting summarized March 23 showed President Bongbong Marcos closing an approval gap against Vice President Sara Duterte as impeachment narratives and administration messaging gained traction, reshaping an earlier lead for the VP.
articles.significance: Public opinion now interacts directly with House timing on impeachment and with 2028 horse-race speculation.
articles.diasporaImpact: OFW chatter and influencer takes on the Marcos–Duterte split increasingly feed into remittance households’ political cues.

articles.headline: DOH Undersecretary Tie and ₱141M in Infrastructure Contracts Draw Scrutiny
articles.descriptionHtml: Watchdog attention focused on a Department of Health undersecretary’s relative winning roughly ₱141 million in infrastructure contracts, sparking conflict-of-interest questions and opposition calls for legislative inquiry even before formal cases are filed.
articles.significance: Health-sector procurement integrity matters acutely when pandemic-era infrastructure and rural hospital upgrades remain politically salient.
articles.diasporaImpact: Families financing care back home lose confidence when hospital projects look captured by insiders.

articles.headline: Lenten Observances Continue Toward Holy Week
articles.descriptionHtml: Catholic-majority communities marked the latter Sundays of Lent, with parishes scheduling reflections, outreach, and charity drives that often double as aid channels during concurrent fuel and food-price stress.
articles.significance: Religious civil society remains a parallel safety net when state cash aid cannot cover everyone.
articles.diasporaImpact: Parish drives and family remittances often spike during Lenten charity seasons for communities abroad and at home.

stories.title: Gulf Crisis Still Weighs on OFW Deployment Conversation
stories.bodyHtml: Reporting March 23 noted Middle East conflict pressure on foreign-policy and labor channels, with DOLE and OWWA monitoring for any fresh deployment or repatriation moves while the peso touched roughly ₱60.34/USD. Families should keep passports, contracts, and OWWA registrations current.

stories.title: Transport Cash Aid Complements — Not Replaces — Remittances
stories.bodyHtml: DSWD’s ₱5,000 payouts for TNVS, jeepney, rider, and similar workers ease one slice of the oil shock; OFW remittances still anchor household budgets where recipients are not in formal target lists.

travelAdvisories.title: Active Travel Advisories — ELEVATED
items: Deployment Suspended: Israel, Lebanon, Iran, Jordan – Active military conflict; airspace closures
Heightened Alert: UAE, Bahrain, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait – Warlike Operations Area declared
Exercise Caution: Myanmar, Cambodia, Laos – Scam hub operations targeting OFWs
Monitoring: US, Europe – Stricter immigration enforcement under anti-migrant policies

items.title: Marcos-Romualdez
items.bodyHtml: DSWD ₱5K transport aid rollout; toll discounts for PUVs; survey momentum vs. VP Duterte as impeachment hearings proceed. WPS: condemned PLA fire-control radar targeting of BRP Miguel Malvar; PCG denied secret China deal reports.

items.title: Duterte
items.bodyHtml: VP Sara faces March 25 House “mini-trial” phase; answer filed March 16 but House says merits unaddressed; late-week angles include Senate dismissal motions. Rodrigo Duterte: ICC confirmation decision expected ~late April; detention appeal lost March 6.

items.title: Marcos-Romualdez (economic)
items.bodyHtml: Oil-driven inflation and a weak peso force layered responses — toll discounts, AICS cash transfers, and loud legislative debate on fuel taxes — defining governability during a twin external shock.
